data.title: Cricut (CRCT) Margin Improvement Challenges Bearish Earnings Narratives After FY 2025 Results
data.description:
Cricut (CRCT) just wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of US$203.6 million and basic EPS of US$0.04, alongside net income of US$7.8 million, setting the tone for how the full year is being read by the market. The company has seen quarterly revenue shift from US$209.3 million with EPS of US$0.06 in Q4 2024 to US$203.6 million with EPS of US$0.04 in Q4 2025. Trailing twelve month EPS sits at US$0.36 on revenue of US$708.8 million, and that mix of steady top line and improving profitability metrics is putting the focus squarely on margins and how durable they look from here.

See our full analysis for Cricut.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to set them against the main stories investors have been telling about Cricut, and see where the fresh margin and earnings trends back those narratives or push against them.

Margins Steady At 10.8% On TTM Basis

  • On a trailing twelve month view, Cricut converted US$708.8 million of revenue into US$76.7 million of net income, which works out to a 10.8% net margin versus 8.8% a year earlier in the dataset.
  • What stands out for a bullish take is that this margin level sits alongside trailing EPS of US$0.36, yet the broader data also shows earnings having declined by 25% per year over the last five years, so any bullish claim about a clean turnaround in profitability has to reckon with that longer multi year track record.

22.1% Earnings Growth Against Five Year Decline

  • The risk and reward summary flags a 22.1% earnings increase over the last year, while over the past five years earnings fell by 25% per year on average, so the recent improvement sits against a much weaker long run trend.
  • Bears argue that the multi year earnings decline is the key signal, and that one stronger year is not enough on its own, which is where the numbers pull in two directions:
    • On one hand, trailing net income of US$76.7 million is higher than the US$62.8 million level a year earlier in the dataset, which supports the idea that profitability has recently been firmer.
    • On the other hand, revenue expectations of 0.5% growth per year versus a 10.2% US market benchmark show why critics still point to limited top line momentum even as recent profit metrics look better.

data.title: STAAR Surgical (STAA) Posts US$80.4 Million Annual Loss Testing Turnaround Narratives
data.description:
Advertisement

STAAR Surgical FY 2025 earnings snapshot

STAAR Surgical (STAA) has wrapped up FY 2025 with Q4 revenue of US$57.8 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.37, keeping the focus firmly on the path back to profitability. The company has seen quarterly revenue range from US$42.6 million to US$94.7 million since the start of 2024, while basic EPS has swung between a loss of US$1.10 and a profit of US$0.20. This sets up a mixed picture on margins that investors may view as an ongoing turnaround story rather than a completed one.

See our full analysis for STAAR Surgical.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the widely followed narratives around growth, profitability and the timing of any margin recovery.

See what the community is saying about STAAR Surgical

Losses still heavy at US$80.4 million over the year

  • On a trailing 12 month basis to Q4 2025, STAAR Surgical reported total revenue of US$239.4 million and a net loss of US$80.4 million, with basic EPS at a loss of US$1.62.
  • Bears highlight that losses have been expanding over the past five years, and the latest trailing figures still show negative earnings. They argue this keeps execution risk front and center even as quarterly revenue has ranged between US$42.6 million and US$94.7 million.
    • Critics point to the 57.6% annual loss growth rate over five years and the FY 2025 net loss of US$80.4 million as evidence that profitability has not yet taken hold, challenging any idea that the turnaround is already reflected in the financials.
    • They also note that basic EPS flipped between profits in Q3 2025 and Q3 2024 and losses in the surrounding quarters. To them, this suggests that earnings are still volatile around a loss making base rather than steadily moving higher.
If you are trying to decide whether that kind of loss profile justifies the current risk, it can help to read how skeptics frame the downside case in detail. 🐻 STAAR Surgical Bear Case

12.1% revenue growth vs market supports the bull case

  • Over the last 12 months, revenue growth of 12.1% per year has outpaced the 10.2% per year benchmark for the US market, even though the company remained loss making on a trailing basis.
  • Bulls argue that this above market top line growth, combined with forecasts that earnings could grow 91.38% per year and reach profitability within three years, heavily supports a turnaround thesis even while trailing 12 month basic EPS is a loss of US$1.62.
    • Supporters point out that quarterly revenue reached US$94.7 million in Q3 2025 versus US$42.6 million at the low end in FY 2025. They see that range as evidence that the product set can support higher volumes when conditions are favorable.
    • They also anchor on the forecasted move from an LTM net loss of US$80.4 million to positive earnings, viewing the combination of 12.1% revenue growth and the 91.38% earnings growth forecast as a sign that operating leverage could improve if revenue continues to build.
If you are wondering how optimistic investors join the dots between this revenue growth, margin expectations and future profits, the full bull case lays that out in one place. 🐂 STAAR Surgical Bull Case

This leads some to question whether the premium P/S reflects growth expectations, or whether the modeled fair value is generous given trailing 12 month losses of US$80.4 million.
    • On one side, the 4x P/S versus 0.8x for peers signals that the market is already assigning STAAR a premium for its 12.1% revenue growth rate.
    • On the other, the DCF fair value of roughly US$39.50 compared with the US$19.22 share price suggests the modeled cash flow path is considerably more optimistic than what the current LTM loss profile might imply.

data.title: Stevanato Group (NYSE:STVN) Margin Improvement To 12% Tests Bullish Earnings Narratives
data.description:
Advertisement

Stevanato Group FY 2025 Earnings Snapshot

Stevanato Group (NYSE:STVN) has put another solid quarter on the board for FY 2025, with Q3 revenue of €303.2 million and basic EPS of €0.13, alongside trailing twelve month EPS of €0.52 on €1.17 billion of revenue, capped by 22.6% earnings growth over the past year. The company has seen revenue move from €259.6 million in Q2 2024 to €303.2 million in Q3 2025, while quarterly EPS has ranged between €0.08 and €0.18 over that period, setting up a picture of higher trailing earnings and a current net margin of 12% that gives investors a cleaner read on underlying profitability.

See our full analysis for Stevanato Group.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to weigh them against the stories already built around Stevanato, and see where the data backs up those narratives and where it starts to push back.

See what the community is saying about Stevanato Group

Margins Improve With 12% Net Profit

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, Stevanato earned €140.6 million of net income on €1.17b of revenue, which works out to a 12% net margin compared with 10.5% a year earlier.
  • Supporters of the bullish view point to this higher margin as evidence that the shift toward higher value products is working, while the data also shows that earnings growth of 22.6% over the past year is much faster than the 3.6% annualized rate over five years. You need to judge whether this quicker pace is a one off or something that can last.
    • The bullish narrative expects margins to continue rising, helped by capacity ramp at sites like Fishers and Latina. The current 12% net margin sets a higher base for those expectations.
    • At the same time, the gap between recent 22.6% earnings growth and the longer term 3.6% trend gives you a clear checkpoint to compare against those bullish margin and earnings targets over time.

Bulls argue that Stevanato's latest 12% margin and faster earnings growth could be the early stages of a higher profit phase, not just a good year. 🐂 Stevanato Group Bull Case

Valuation Sits Below Sector P/E

  • The shares change hands at a trailing P/E of 29.2x, compared with a peer average of 56.6x and a US Life Sciences industry average of 35.1x. A DCF fair value of €17.82 sits slightly above the current price of €17.50.
  • Bears focus on the risk that, even with this lower P/E, expectations are already rich because the analyst price target of €27.51 is well above today’s price. That target assumes earnings growth of about 22.6% to 22.7% per year and margins rising from around 11.8% to the mid teens.
    • Critics highlight that the recent five year earnings growth rate of 3.6% per year is far below the earnings growth that is being projected, so the valuation still leans on a much stronger future than the longer term history reflects.
    • They also point out that revenue is forecast to grow 9.7% per year, slightly below the 10.2% US market forecast. A higher earnings growth path would therefore need to come mainly from margins and mix rather than from faster sales growth than the broader market.

Skeptics warn that even with a P/E discount, the share price at €17.50 still depends on growth stepping up from the 3.6% five year earnings trend. 🐻 Stevanato Group Bear Case

Earnings Momentum Outruns Revenue Pace

  • Quarterly revenue moved from €259.6 million in Q2 2024 to €303.2 million in Q3 2025, while trailing twelve month revenue reached €1.17b and earnings over that same period grew 22.6% with revenue growth forecasts at 9.7% per year.
  • The consensus narrative flags that a lot of the story is about earnings quality and mix, not just sales volume. The numbers back that up because net margin rose from 10.5% to 12% while revenue is only expected to grow slightly below the wider US market, so any future upside would likely rely more on product mix, pricing, and efficiency than on outpacing the market on revenue growth alone.
    • Analysts looking for higher earnings see the current margin and revenue mix as a sign that high value solutions are already pulling more weight in the income statement.
    • More cautious readers might instead treat the difference between earnings growth and revenue growth as something to monitor closely, in case margin gains slow while revenue growth stays in the high single digits.

data.title: Propel Bank Launch And Freshline Expansion Test Propel Holdings Growth Story
data.description:
• Propel Holdings launched Propel Bank and entered a new partnership with Column to expand its lending reach to underserved customers.
  • The company introduced Freshline, a new platform aimed at serving additional segments that have limited access to traditional credit.
  • Propel secured a US$60 million forward flow receivables agreement with Mesirow to support the funding needs of these expanded products.

For investors watching TSX:PRL, these moves come as the stock trades around CA$20.43, with a return of 201.9% over three years but a 23.0% decline over the past year. In the shorter term, the share price has seen a 6.0% decline over the past week and a 21.0% decline over the past month, which places this operational shift against a more volatile recent trading backdrop.

The combination of Propel Bank, the Column relationship, Freshline, and the Mesirow funding line indicates a broader product toolkit aimed at underserved borrowers in more regions. For readers, a key consideration is how effectively TSX:PRL can translate these initiatives into stable receivables growth and risk management over time, and whether the current share price already reflects this wider business model.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Propel Holdings by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Propel Holdings.

We've flagged 3 risks for Propel Holdings. See which could impact your investment.

This set of moves gives you a clearer sense of where Propel wants to go as a lender to underserved borrowers. Propel Bank and the Column partnership add more control over funding and distribution, while Freshline targets new customer segments and regions without relying solely on the existing MoneyKey and CreditFresh brands. The US$60 million forward flow agreement with Mesirow means a third party will buy receivables from Freshline, which can help Propel scale originations without tying up as much of its own balance sheet.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Propel Holdings Narrative

  • The launch of Propel Bank and the Freshline program lines up with the narrative around geographic expansion, product diversification, and scaling Lending as a Service, all aimed at broadening revenue sources.
  • At the same time, management has flagged higher credit costs and start up expenses, and these new programs could pressure margins if credit performance or volumes do not develop as planned.
  • The bank license in Puerto Rico and the deeper use of a partner such as Column introduce regulatory and counterparty elements that are not fully captured in the high level growth discussion of the existing narrative.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Propel Holdings to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Analysts have noted that Propel’s debt is not well covered by operating cash flow, which can matter as the company supports new lending programs and bank operations.
  • ⚠️ The fourth quarter showed lower net income of US$5.94 million versus US$11.61 million a year earlier, reflecting higher credit costs and start up spending that could continue as Freshline and Propel Bank scale.
  • 🎁 Historically, earnings have been growing and analysts have discussed further earnings growth potential, which could be supported if Propel Bank, Column and Freshline add new fee and interest income streams.
  • 🎁 The Mesirow forward flow agreement may allow Propel to grow receivables with less direct capital strain, which can help manage balance sheet risk while still pursuing expansion.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to watch how quickly Freshline volumes build, and whether credit losses on those loans track in line with the rest of the portfolio. The interaction between Propel Bank and existing programs will also matter, especially how much it lowers funding costs or opens doors to new partners compared with traditional non bank lenders such as Enova, OneMain or LendingClub. Finally, pay attention to how credit costs, operating expenses and dividend payments show up in future quarters, given the recent drop in quarterly profit alongside record full year revenue.

Losses Narrowing Over Five Years

  • Supporters of the bullish view argue that this multi year improvement in losses, combined with Cryoport backing roughly 70% of cell and gene clinical trials, sets the company up for stronger earnings power over time. However, the latest quarterly net losses between US$8.7 million and US$11.7 million through FY 2025 show that the path to profitability is still very much a work in progress.
    • The bullish narrative highlights potential margin gains from services like IntegriCell and facility investments. At the same time, the reported trailing loss of US$42.0 million underlines how much margin expansion would be needed to reach those outcomes.

National Vision Holdings (EYE) has put up a mixed FY 2025 scorecard so far, with Q3 revenue at US$487.3 million, basic EPS of US$0.04 and same store sales growth of 7.7%, set against a trailing twelve month loss of US$3.2 million and basic EPS of roughly US$0.04 loss. Basic EPS has moved from a Q4 2024 loss of US$0.37 per share to a Q1 2025 profit of US$0.18 per share and then back to US$0.04 in Q3 2025, with same store sales growth running between 0.9% and 7.7%.   • Same store sales growth has moved from 0.9% in Q3 2024 to 5.5% in Q1 2025, 5.9% in Q2, and 7.7% in Q3, while net income swung from a loss of US$29.4 million in Q4 2024 to profits of US$14.2 million, US$8.7 million, and US$3.4 million in the first three quarters of 2025.
  • Bulls point to this combination of higher comps and better profitability as evidence that things like premium frames and managed care expansion are working. However, the trailing twelve month picture still shows a net income loss of US$3.2 million, which means the bullish idea of comp driven earnings expansion is only partly reflected in the latest numbers so far.

Still unprofitable over the last 12 months

  • On a trailing twelve month basis to Q3 2025, National Vision generated US$1.9b of revenue but recorded a net income loss of US$3.2 million and basic EPS of roughly a US$0.04 loss, with five year earnings having declined at about 66.2% per year.
  • Bears highlight this longer term earnings decline as a key concern. The latest data lines up with that, because even after three profitable quarters in 2025 the trailing twelve month figures remain in the red and show that the recent improvement has not yet been enough to offset earlier losses.
    • The trailing twelve month net loss narrowed from US$27.2 million at Q4 2024 to US$3.2 million at Q3 2025, so the direction has improved, but the fact that earnings are still negative fits the cautious view that profitability is fragile.
    • Critics also point to the five year annual earnings decline of 66.2% as a reminder that the business has faced sustained pressure on profit metrics. This matters if you are weighing how durable the recent quarterly profits might be.

However, the mix of a share price above DCF fair value and a P/S that is rich to the industry but low versus peers creates tension between growth expectations and what the current fundamentals, including trailing losses, actually show.
    • Supporters who focus on analysts expecting margins to rise from a loss position to mid single digit levels need to weigh that against the fact that trailing twelve month net income is still a US$3.2 million loss.
    • At the same time, anyone comparing multiples will see that the 1.2x P/S already prices the stock above the broader specialty retail group while the company remains loss making over the year. This may prompt questions about how quickly earnings need to develop to support that multiple.

data.title: Capital Power Q3 Margin Softness And One Off Gain Test Bullish Growth Narratives
data.description:
Capital Power (TSX:CPX) has reported another busy quarter, with Q3 2025 revenue of CA$1.2b and basic EPS of CA$0.94, compared with a trailing twelve-month figure showing revenue of CA$3.4b and EPS of CA$2.65. Over recent periods, the company has seen quarterly revenue move between CA$407m and CA$1.2b, while basic EPS has ranged from a loss of CA$0.92 in Q2 2025 to CA$1.76 in Q4 2024. With net profit margins at 11.4% over the last year, down from 13.7%, the latest results present an earnings profile supported by a sizeable one-off gain alongside a thinner underlying margin picture.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to consider how this earnings profile aligns with the widely followed stories about Capital Power, and where those narratives may look either reinforced or out of sync.

See what the community is saying about Capital Power

Advertisement

Margins soften with 11.4% net return

  • Over the last 12 months, Capital Power converted CA$3.4b of revenue into CA$383 million of net income, which works out to an 11.4% net margin compared with 13.7% a year earlier.
  • Analysts' consensus view expects earnings to grow about 15.35% per year while revenue is forecast to grow around 2.7% per year. This creates a bullish narrative that margin pressure could ease over time, yet the current 11.4% margin and reliance on a CA$278 million one off gain challenge the idea that earnings strength is already firmly in place.
    • Consensus narrative leans on diversified growth and operational upgrades to support more stable cash flows. However, the step down from 13.7% to 11.4% shows profitability has recently moved in the opposite direction.
    • Bears also highlight rising financing costs and competitive pressure as potential headwinds. This ties back to the weaker margin in the latest 12 month period and suggests the forecasted earnings growth needs careful scrutiny against these risks.

Interest and dividend coverage flagged as weak

  • Recent analysis points out that interest coverage is weak and the 4.49% dividend is not covered by earnings or free cash flow, which means current payouts lean heavily on future improvement rather than trailing cash generation.
  • Bears argue that rising financing costs and the need to fund growth could squeeze cash flows, and the weak coverage metrics give that bearish view real numbers behind it.
    • Critics highlight that if earnings and free cash flow do not catch up with the 4.49% dividend, management may have fewer options when balancing debt service, reinvestment, and distributions.
    • At the same time, consensus expects earnings growth of about 15.35% per year, so the key tension is whether that growth is enough to improve coverage faster than financing costs and investment needs build up.
Skeptics point to these coverage gaps as a reason to stress test your own cash flow assumptions before leaning on the dividend story. 🐻 Capital Power Bear Case

P/E of 25.1x against mixed valuation signals

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 25.1x, higher than the Global Renewable Energy industry average of 16.7x but slightly below the peer average of 27.6x, while the current price of CA$61.53 sits below the cited analyst target of CA$74.54 and well under the CA$143.05 DCF fair value.
  • Bullish investors often point to this gap to the CA$143.05 DCF fair value and the analyst target as support for upside. Yet the 25.1x P/E and softer profitability keep that bullish case grounded in a trade off.
    • What stands out is that the stock is priced above the broader industry on P/E even though net margin is 11.4% and includes a sizeable one off gain. This indicates that a portion of that valuation still rests on forward growth assumptions.
    • On the other side, consensus expects earnings growth of around 15.35% per year, which, if achieved, would help the current P/E look more reasonable relative to peers. The central question is how comfortably you view those forecasts versus the recent margin and coverage data.
